narrative:
President Trump announced a new retirement savings proposal during his State of the Union address on February 24, 2026, targeting millions of Americans without employer-sponsored retirement plans. [1][2][3]

## Key Proposal Details

Trump stated that "half of all working Americans still do not have access to a retirement plan with matching contributions from an employer" and announced his administration would provide these workers "access to the same type of retirement plan offered to every federal worker" with government matching contributions of "up to $1,000 each year." [1][2][3]

## Scale and Target Population

Sources provide varying estimates of affected workers. CNBC reports "roughly 56 million Americans lack access to an employer-sponsored retirement plan at work" according to 2025 Pew Charitable Trusts research [1], while Daily Wire cites "about 40.6 million full-time working Americans lack access to a retirement plan" with "nearly 51% [not benefiting] from an employer match." [2] Axios estimates "roughly 50 million Americans without employer-sponsored retirement plans." [3]

## Implementation Framework

The proposed accounts would function similarly to the federal Thrift Savings Plan (TSP), offering portable accounts that workers can maintain when changing jobs. [1][2] The White House plans to leverage existing legislation, specifically the Saver's Match provision from SECURE 2.0 that takes effect in 2027, which provides "a 50% matching contribution from Uncle Sam on up to $2,000 in annual retirement savings" for workers under certain income thresholds. [1][3]

## Legislative Path and Funding

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent suggested the law could pass through reconciliation, similar to "the One Big Beautiful Bill Act." [1] However, sources note implementation challenges: Daily Wire reports that "critics argue existing legislation will not allow the plan to come to fruition, while the White House says it is possible but acknowledges future legislation would be needed to 'bolster this game-changing plan.'" [2]

## Private Investment Component

Both Daily Wire and Axios report that private investors and philanthropists would be able to contribute to these accounts, following the model used for "investment accounts for children and the White House ballroom project." [2][3] Daily Wire notes that "Trump Accounts for kids have also had a healthy dose of private investment, with Michael and Susan Dell kicking off donations with $6.25 billion." [2]

## Expert Analysis

Teresa Ghilarducci, a professor at The New School, called Trump's proposal "a recognition of reality" [1] and "a big deal" that "finally recognizes that most people don't have anything saved for retirement." [3] She revealed she "worked on an early version of this plan with White House economic adviser Kevin Hassett in 2021" as an effort to "get someone far on the right and far on the left to come together." [3]

citations.title: Trump pitches new retirement plan with a federal match of up to $1,000 per year — who could benefit
citations.quote: Half of all of working Americans still do not have access to a retirement plan with matching contributions from an employer. To remedy this gross disparity, I'm announcing that next year my administration will give these often-forgotten American workers … access to the same type of retirement plan offered to every federal worker. We will match your contribution with up to $1,000 each year

citations.title: Trump's Primetime SOTU Surprise Could Reshape Retirement Savings
citations.quote: About 40.6 million full-time working Americans lack access to a retirement plan, and nearly 51% do not benefit from an employer match

citations.title: Trump announces new retirement plan at State of the Union
citations.quote: This is a big deal. It finally recognizes that most people don't have anything saved for retirement, and they don't save consistently

keyDevelopments: Trump announced new retirement savings proposal during February 24, 2026 State of the Union address
Plan targets 40-56 million Americans without employer-sponsored retirement plans
Proposal offers up to $1,000 annual government matching contributions
Accounts would be modeled after federal Thrift Savings Plan (TSP)
Implementation would leverage existing SECURE 2.0 Saver's Match provision taking effect in 2027
Treasury Secretary suggests using reconciliation process for passage
Private philanthropic contributions would be permitted
White House acknowledges additional legislation may be needed for full implementation
